coredata.dc:title: Green synthetic carbon quantum dots based on waste tobacco leaves and its application to detecting borax content in flour and its products
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Chunyu
Du, Jialing
Zhao, Hanqing
Xiong, Zhili
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared using discarded tobacco leaves as the precursor material combined. And the method used in our experiment is a green, low-cost, and simple one-step hydrothermal method. The structural morphology, optical properties, and fundamental elemental composition of these CQDs were characterized via transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction spectrum (XRD), etc. A fluorescence probe was developed with these CQDs for borax determination. Influencing factors including solution salt concentration, interfering substances, and solution pH on the detection of borax were investigated. The fluorescence enhancement degree of these CQDs had a linear relationship with different concentrations of borax (0.025–5 mmol·L-1), r2 = 0.9961, and based on the formula 3σ/S, σ represents the standard deviation of the detection data for 11 blank samples, S refers to the slope of the standard curve, LOD = 8.0 µmol·L-1. Furthermore, we also initially elucidated the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of CQDs. Satisfactory results were obtained for borax determination in flour, bread, and instant noodle samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Functional carbon quantum dots
Carbon materials
Nanoparticles
Food safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability of CQDs and optimization of reaction system conditions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University ( ZQN2016011 ), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department ( 2020LZD02 ), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province ( 2020-181 ), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The fluorescence spectrum of CQDs excited from 330–400 nm (b) The XRD imaging the CQDs (c) TEM of CQDS (d) The particle size distribution of CQDs (e) FT-IR of CQDs.
(a) The full-scan XPS spectrum of the CQDs (b) The high resolution XPS of C1s (c)The high resolution XPS of O1s (d) The high resolution XPS of N1s.
(a) UV absorption spectra of CQDs in water, ethanol, and acetonitrile (b) fluorescence spectra of CQDs in water, ethanol, and acetonitrile.

(a) Effect of pH on the fluorescence strength of the CQDs (b) Effect of illumination on fluorescence strength of CQDs (c) Effect of NaCl concentration on the fluorescence strength of the CQDs (d) Effect of incubation temperatures on the fluorescence strength of the CQDs.
(a) Effect of pH on the fluorescence strength of the CQDs+Borax (b) Effect of reaction time on fluorescence strength of CQDs+Borax.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 6

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The bar graph shows changes in fluorescence intensity brought by different substances (b) Fluorescence profiles of CQDs in the presence of borax (c) The linear equation for the borax.

ce:figure.ce:source: (F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of blank CQDs, F as the fluorescence intensity of CQDs after adding different concentration borax).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Zeta potential distribution of CQDs, CQDs+Borax (b)Fluorescence emission decay curve (c) Schematic representation of preparation, analysis and application of the CQDs.
Determination results of borax in food samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Green synthetic carbon quantum dots based on waste tobacco leaves and its application to detecting borax content in flour and its products

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenhe District, Shenyang, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenhe District, Shenyang, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenhe District, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared using discarded tobacco leaves as the precursor material combined. And the method used in our experiment is a green, low-cost, and simple one-step hydrothermal method. The structural morphology, optical properties, and fundamental elemental composition of these CQDs were characterized via transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction spectrum (XRD), etc. A fluorescence probe was developed with these CQDs for borax determination. Influencing factors including solution salt concentration, interfering substances, and solution pH on the detection of borax were investigated. The fluorescence enhancement degree of these CQDs had a linear relationship with different concentrations of borax (0.025–5 mmol·L-1), r2 = 0.9961, and based on the formula 3σ/S, σ represents the standard deviation of the detection data for 11 blank samples, S refers to the slope of the standard curve, LOD = 8.0 µmol·L-1. Furthermore, we also initially elucidated the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of CQDs. Satisfactory results were obtained for borax determination in flour, bread, and instant noodle samples.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Food additives can improve the quality and properties of food, food producers usually use them in the manufacturing, processing, transportation, and storage of food. Because some food additives have potential risks to human health, they have been strictly banned by some national laws and regulations. Unfortunately, some sellers are still stubborn about adding illegal food additives (Borax, Sudan, Dichlorvos, et al.) for even more income. Adding borax to food will increase the taste (toughness, crispness, elasticity, et al.) and increase the food storage time. However, some studies [1,2] have shown that borax causes various symptoms such as vomiting, fatigue, and renal failure; and it can cause the proliferation of immune cells and induce chromosome exchange in human chromosomes. To date, various methods had been established for borax detection, (Such as the turmeric test paper method [3] and IR [4] et al.). The above technologies more or less have some disadvantages such as complicated operation, troublesome sample preparation, expensive equipment, et al. Therefore, it is essential to monitor the borax in foods with a simple, fast and effective method aiming to ensure food safety and consumer confidence.

In recent years, scientists had synthesized spherical zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterial (average particle size: r < 10 nm) named carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are supposed to be an eminent member of the nanocarbon family [5,6]. CQDs act as a class of quasi-spherical nanoparticles consisting of carbon nuclei and surface groups. The oxygen groups on the surface that make CQDs possess biocompatibility and good solubilities in water, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl. Furthermore, it is supposed to be a class of environmentally friendly materials with the traits of Good electrical conductivity, high chemical stability, optical stability, low toxicity, and good photoluminescence (PL) properties [7,8]. There is enormous attention attached to the applications of photocatalysis [9–11], drug delivery [12,13], bio-imaging [14], sensors [15] because of the above advantages.

In comparison with the traditional detection methods, fluorescence-based analytical methods have shown great potential in the field of portable sensors and detection. It has engaged lots of attention recently due to their high sensitivity, fast response, and convenient operation [16]. Fluorescent probes are an integral part of fluorescent biosensors converting the recognition process into a fluorescent signal that can be easily detected and measured. Since fluorescence technology relies on the output of the measured substance determined by the probe and labeled by the time signal, the realization of the high sensitivity of fluorescence analysis technology mainly depends on the magnification of the fluorescence signal [17,18]. Thus, on account of the excellent fluorescent characteristic of CQDs, they have the potential to be employed in various fields. In addition, because of the eminent biocompatibility and easy surface modifications of CQDs, they are considered one of the most powerful carbon nanomaterials [19,20].

At present, CQDs are wildly used to construct fluorescent probes in food safety detection fields. Moreover, since CQDs are known as environmentally friendly nanomaterials and the introduction of the green concept and green chemistry [21], it is important to select green cheap materials and environmentally friendly methods for synthesizing stable and functional CQDs. Biomass is considered to be the raw material for many organic compounds due to its advantages such as rich yield, renewable, and rich in carbon elements. The hydrothermal method, is a powerful approach based on biomass carbon source precursors, revealing great preponderances of simple, sustainable, and green. There are many reports on the synthesis of CQDs using the hydrothermal method. So it is feasible that use biomass resources for the green synthesis of CQDs by hydrothermal method. Zhang et al. Synthesized efficient CQDs from ginkgo kernels by a hydrothermal reaction, which was applied in medical imaging and detection of toxins in food [22]. ELANGO D et al. Utilized a waste shrimp shell for the sustainable synthesis of CQDs via the one-step hydrothermal method and the prepared S-CQDs have spherical structures with good fluorescent stability and biocompatibility [23]. It is feasible that use biomass resources for green synthesis of CQDs by hydrothermal method.

In this experiment, we used a simple, green, and economy friendly economy-friendly hydrothermal method to prepare the CQDs whose precursor material is discarded tobacco leaves. A fluorescence analysis method for detecting borax in flour and its products had established. The fluorescence of the CQDs was significantly enhanced with varying concentrations of borax added. According to the results of the literature review, this is the first study of CQD preparation using discarded tobacco leaves. The fluorescence analysis method was established based on the CQDs to detect borax.

Tobacco leaves and food samples (flour, bread, and instant noodle) were purchased from a local marketplace in Shenyang, Liaoning, China. The purified water was purchased from Hangzhou Wahaha Group Co, Ltd(Hangzhou, China). Sodium chloride, magnesium chloride, potassium chloride, calcium chloride, zinc chloride, and sodium carboxymethylcellulose (CMCNa) were purchased from Tianjin Behrens Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Vitamin A and vitamin B6 were obtained from Shanghai McLin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Borax was gained by Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China).

Ultrasound was performed on the KQ5200 ultrasonic cleaner (Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Ltd.). The admixture was heated in the 101 −1AB draft drying cabinet. (Tianjin TAISITE Instrument Co., Ltd). The mixed solution was centrifuged by the LC-400 low-speed centrifuge (USTC Chuangxin Co., Ltd.Zonkia Branch). The freeze-drying operated on LYOQUEST-85 lyophilizer (Azbil Telstar Technologies S. L. U., Spain). The fluorescence spectra were recorded with an F97 spectrophotometer (Shanghai Cold Light Technology Co., Ltd., China). The morphological structures of CQDs were analyzed using a Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL Company, Japan). The samples were coated with a thin film before TEM analysis and the images were taken under a high vacuum (JEM-2100, JEOL Company, Japan). The crystallographic structures of the materials were characterized by XRD (China Booker Technology Co., Ltd.), using filtered Cu Kα radiation with an accelerating voltage of 40 kV and a current of 40 mA. Samples had been prepared by pressed-disk technique and performed XPS testing (Therma Fisher, USA). FT-IR spectra were recorded with a Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Therma Fisher Scientific, USA) in the range of 4000-400 cm-1 by the KBr pellet method.

In a typical synthesis, we took an appropriate amount of tobacco leaves to crush and 0.4 g of processed tobacco leaves were added to 40 mL of pure water. Then, the admixture was loaded into a 100 mL autoclave in the oven, and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature. The solution in the autoclave was centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 10 min. Then the brown solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane filter and then dialyzed against deionized water for 24 h to obtain CQDs. The resulting pure CQDs solution was freeze-dried or preserved at −4 °C for use.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the CQDs have wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission properties, and their emission peak position is red-shifted from 436 nm to 495 nm with the increased excitation wavelength. This phenomenon is named excitation-wavelength dependence. The reason for this phenomenon may be the “giant red-edge effect” [24], which was interpreted as: Fluorophore dipoles such as oxygen-containing groups (such as -OH, COC, etc.) on the surface of CQDs. When the fluorescence lifetime of these fluorophore dipoles is close to the relaxation efficiency of the solvent dipole, the excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence phenomenon occurs. Fluorescence quantum yield is measured by integrating sphere and steady-state/transient fluorescence spectroscopy.
Calculation
formula
:
Abs-QY =
L sample
÷ (
E control
−
E sample
)
[ 25 ]

In the formula, L sample is the emission intensity of the sample, and E control and E sample are the excitation light intensity that is not absorbed by the control and sample, respectively. The calculated result: QY = 11%. As shown in the Fig. 1b, the CQDs have an XRD characteristic peak at 21.23°, which corresponds to the (002) face of the graphite [26].

The TEM image (Fig. 1c) indicated that the CQDs have good dispersibility. All the CQDs scanned are globular, with a particle size of 10.4 – 17.9 nm, and the average diameter is 14.2 nm (Fig. 1d). Radical groups of the CQDs are validated with FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 1e, the peaks at 3388 and 1710/1619 cm-1 are attributed to OH, NH, and CO stretch vibrations [27,28], respectively. And the peaks at 2644 and 1386 cm-1 are ascribed to -CH3 and -CH bending vibration, and the peaks at about 1092 cm-1 are caused by COC stretch vibration [29]. FT-IR spectra result presented above shows that the CQDs surface was filled with -OH and -COOH. These hydrophilic groups ensure the CQDs have easy water dispersion and good fluorescence properties.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, full-scan XPS of the CQDs is shown the characteristic peaks of C, N, and O at 285.2 eV, 399.7 and 531.3 eV. Fig. 2b are shown a high-resolution XPS map of the C1s. The peaks at 283.60 eV, 284.99 eV, and 287.00 eV belong to CC/CC, CN, and CO [28], respectively. Fig. 2c shows the high-resolution XPS map of N1s, and the three peaks at 400.36 eV, 399.45 eV, and 398.75 eV are classified as the characteristic peaks of -NH2, CN, and CN [31], respectively. The high-resolution XPS map of the O1s is shown in the Fig. 2d. The three peaks at 531.5 eV, 531.9 eV, and 532.5 eV were classified as the characteristic peaks of -OH, CO and-COO [29,30], respectively. In addition, by analyzing the relative content of the above four elements with XPS, it showed that the relative atomic content of C, N, and O in the CQDs was 68.83%, 6.39%, and 24.78%, respectively. Combining results combining FT-IR and XPS show that the surface of the CQDs has functional groups such as -OH, CO, -COO. These functional groups make the CQDs better water-soluble.

In addition, it was found that CQDs have excellent solubility in water, ethanol, and acetonitrile and have shown solvent effects in their SPEF spectra. CQDs were dissolved in water, ethanol, acetonitrile, and UV absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were collected. As shown in Fig. 3 a, there was no significant effect on UV absorption spectra of CQDs in different solvents. It has broadband absorption in the range of 250 nm-300 nm, which is formed by the π-π* transition of sp 2; the variation trend is completely consistent. But for fluorescence spectra(Fig. 3b), with the solvent polarity decreasing, the emission of CQDs exhibits red shift from 435 nm to 458 nm, showing solvent effect. Further, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs is the highest in water, so it was water that was chosen as the solvent of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of CQDs and optimization of reaction system conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Influencing factors including solution pH, light stability, solution salt concentrations, and incubation temperatures on the stability of CQDs are taken into account. Fig. 4 a shows that when the pH of the solution increase from 2.06 to 10.06 the fluorescence intensity has little change, but reduces clearly with the enlargement of pH. The above results indicate that the CQDs are unstable in a strong alkali solution. As observed in Fig. 4b, with the increase of UV exposure time the fluorescence strength of CQDs fluctuates slightly, showing that the CQDs possess good photobleaching resistance. We investigated the effect of sodium chloride concentration and incubation temperatures on the stability of CQDs (Fig. 4c, d). Fig. 4c shows that with the increase of NaCl concentration the fluorescence intensity of CQDs does not change markedly. It shows that CQDs have good salt concentration stability. Fig. 4d shows that the fluorescence intensity value was kept constant at different temperatures.

This study also optimized the reaction conditions for the system (CQDs+ Borax). The influence of pH in the range 2.06–13.52 was studied to confirm the optimum acidity for further experiment. The result is shown in the Fig. 5 a, with the increase of pH (2.06–13.52) the fluorescence intensity of the system changed and keep it steady in the range of pH 2.06–10.06, and intensity maximum at pH = 7.21. Therefore, we will perform the next experiment in an environment of pH = 7.21. In order to eliminate the interference of the reaction time of CQDs and borax on the measurement results, the fluorescence strength of CQDs was measured after adding borax 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 min, as shown in Fig. 5b, the reaction time had no effect on the change of fluorescence intensity.

To estimate the specificity of this method, the detection of some potential interfering substances, such as CMCCNa, CO3 2−, Mg2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, Zn+, vitamin A(VA), and vitamin B6(VB6), by the as-prepared CDs was investigated. In this experiment, the above-mentioned analytes(10 mM) were mixed with a CQDs solution (0.4 mmol·L−1)respectively and diluted the mixtures with pure water. The volume of these interfering substances and CQDs solution was 1 mL. This was then tested for fluorescence response at λex/λem = 375/460 nm after thoroughly mixed respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 a, borax was found to be sensitive to the CQDs significantly. As the CQDs certified selective detection of borax, the sensitivity was investigated next. And fluorescence emission responses of mixtures of CQDs solution (0.4 mmol·L−1) and borax solutions were measured. As presented in Fig. 6c, the fluorescence strength was increased linearly as the concentration of borax increased from 0.025 to 5 mmol·L−1. In addition, the detection limit (LOD) was calculated as 8.0 µmol·L−1 according to the formula 3 σ/S.

This experiment explored the mechanism of CQDs measuring borax. As shown in Fig. 7 a, after borax is added, the Zeta potential of CQDs increases to −6.47 mV. This phenomenon may be due to the formation of hydrogen bond association between borax's -OH and CQDs surface -COOH and -OH, this leads to a reduced negative charge on the CQDs surface [30] (Fig. 7a). In Fig.7b, the fluorescence life-span of free CQDs is 4.73 ns, the fluorescence life-span increases to 6.01 ns after adding borax. According to previous literature reported [31,32], after borax is added, the CQDs+ borax complex formed makes the surface of CQDs passivated and enhances the reorganization of electrons and holes on the surface of CQDs, which leads to an increase in fluorescence lifetime.

As shown in Table 1 , comparing the method in this study with other detection methods, such as HPLC [33], ICP-OES [34], CP-AES [35], and titration methods [36], it was found that the present method has the advantages of inexpensive instrumentation, simple operation, easy sample pretreatment, fast analysis, wide linear range, and no contamination.

The method has been applied to the determination of borax in flour, bread, and instant noodle samples. A homogeneous mixture of food (10 g) and pure water (50 ml) was centrifuged at 4500 r-min−1 for 10 min to obtain the supernatant. The sample solutions were blended with borax solutions next for fluorescence detection. As shown in Table 2 , according to the fluorescence response of the samples, the recovery results were calculated in the range of 85.2–105.8%. Therefore, the established method is viable for borax determination in the practical sample.

This study is the first time to develop an environment-friendly, the simple hydrothermal method for preparing CQDs using discarded tobacco leaves. As a fluorescence probe, these CQDs enable the detection of borax sensitively and selectively. The feasibility of the method was confirmed by measuring borax in the actual samples with good results. Based on the results of the literature review, we believe this is the first study that uses discarded tobacco leaves to prepare CQDs and use it as fluorescence probes for the determination of borax.

Chunyu Zhou: Conceptualization, Resources, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Validation, Visualization. Jialing Du: Resources, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Hanqing Zhao: Formal analysis, Investigation. Zhili Xiong: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Longshan Zhao: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020-181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).
